title: A Conversation at a Career Fair
summary: A university student named Maya approaches a recruiter named David at a career fair. They discuss the company, the available roles, and what qualities the recruiter looks for in candidates.

body.speaker: Maya
body.text: Hi there. I noticed your company's banner from across the hall. Do you mind if I ask a few questions about the roles you're hiring for?

body.speaker: David
body.text: Not at all — that's exactly why we're here. Welcome. My name's David, and I'm a senior recruiter at Novabridge Solutions. What's your name?

body.speaker: Maya
body.text: I'm Maya. I'm finishing my final year in business administration. I've been looking at graduate programmes, and yours caught my eye online.

body.speaker: David
body.text: Great to meet you, Maya. Our graduate programme is one of the things I'm most proud to talk about. We take on about twenty graduates each year across three tracks — operations, technology, and client services. Do you have a particular area in mind?

body.speaker: Maya
body.text: I'm most interested in client services, actually. I enjoy working directly with people, and I've done a lot of group projects and part-time customer-facing work during my studies.

body.speaker: David
body.text: That's a strong background for client services. Can I ask — what do you feel you've gained from that customer-facing experience specifically?

body.speaker: Maya
body.text: I think the biggest thing is learning to listen carefully before responding. A lot of customers don't always know how to describe what they need, so you have to read between the lines and ask the right follow-up questions.

body.speaker: David
body.text: That's a really mature way to put it. Active listening is something we look for specifically in client services candidates. Many applicants underestimate how important it is compared to, say, technical knowledge.

body.speaker: Maya
body.text: That's reassuring to hear. Can I ask what a typical first year looks like for a graduate in the client services track?

body.speaker: David
body.text: Absolutely. The first three months are an induction period — you shadow experienced account managers, attend internal training sessions, and gradually take on smaller client queries on your own. By month six, most graduates are managing a small portfolio of accounts independently.

body.speaker: Maya
body.text: That sounds like a well-structured start. One thing I wanted to ask is about the company culture. I've read your website, but I'd like to hear it from someone who actually works there.

body.speaker: David
body.text: Honestly, I'd say the culture is collaborative but driven. People here are ambitious, but they're also very willing to help each other out. We don't have a culture of internal competition — it's more about the team hitting shared goals.

body.speaker: Maya
body.text: That's the kind of environment I work best in. I've always found that I produce better results when there's a sense of collective purpose rather than everyone competing for individual recognition.

body.speaker: David
body.text: Then I think you'd fit in well. One last thing — our application closes in three weeks, so if you're serious about applying, I'd encourage you not to leave it too late. The written assessment and video interview take some preparation.

body.speaker: Maya
body.text: Thank you so much, David. This has been really helpful. I'll definitely start the application this week.

body.speaker: David
body.text: Excellent. Here's my card. Feel free to email me if you have any questions once you've started the process. Good luck, Maya.

annotations.meaningVi: thu hút sự chú ý của tôi
annotations.exampleEn: The advertisement caught my eye because of its bright colours.

annotations.meaningVi: chương trình dành cho sinh viên mới tốt nghiệp
annotations.exampleEn: She applied to several graduate programmes in finance.
The company plans to take on fifty new employees next year.

annotations.meaningVi: liên quan trực tiếp đến khách hàng
annotations.exampleEn: A customer-facing role requires strong communication skills.

annotations.meaningVi: đọc hiểu ẩn ý, hiểu điều không được nói rõ
annotations.exampleEn: You need to read between the lines to understand what the client really wants.

annotations.meaningVi: câu hỏi tiếp theo, câu hỏi bổ sung
annotations.exampleEn: She asked several follow-up questions to clarify the requirements.

annotations.meaningVi: lắng nghe chủ động, lắng nghe có tập trung
annotations.exampleEn: Active listening means fully concentrating on what the speaker is saying.
Don't underestimate the importance of first impressions.

annotations.meaningVi: giai đoạn định hướng ban đầu cho nhân viên mới
annotations.exampleEn: The induction period lasts two weeks and covers company policies.

annotations.meaningVi: theo sát để học hỏi, quan sát người có kinh nghiệm làm việc
annotations.exampleEn: New recruits shadow senior staff during their first month.

annotations.meaningVi: danh mục khách hàng được phụ trách
annotations.exampleEn: She manages a portfolio of accounts worth over two million pounds.

annotations.meaningVi: hợp tác nhưng có định hướng và tham vọng
annotations.exampleEn: The team has a collaborative but driven approach to hitting targets.

annotations.meaningVi: mục tiêu chung, mục đích tập thể
annotations.exampleEn: A sense of collective purpose helps teams perform better.
Some people are motivated more by individual recognition than by salary.

annotations.meaningVi: bài kiểm tra viết, bài đánh giá bằng văn bản
annotations.exampleEn: The written assessment tests your reasoning and communication skills.

annotations.meaningVi: hòa nhập, phù hợp với môi trường
annotations.exampleEn: He was nervous at first, but he quickly fit in with the team.

grammarNotes.title: Modal Verbs for Polite Requests and Advice
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong hội thoại này, các modal verb được dùng để tạo giọng điệu lịch sự và chuyên nghiệp. 'Do you mind if I ask' và 'Can I ask' dùng để xin phép một cách lịch sự. 'I'd encourage you not to leave it too late' dùng 'would' để đưa ra lời khuyên nhẹ nhàng, tránh nghe quá mệnh lệnh. Ở B1, học viên cần phân biệt: 'can' cho phép thông thường, 'could' / 'would you mind' cho yêu cầu lịch sự hơn.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Modal verbs are used throughout this dialogue to create a polite, professional tone. 'Do you mind if I ask' and 'Can I ask' are polite permission structures. 'I'd encourage you not to leave it too late' uses 'would' to soften advice. At B1, learners should distinguish between 'can' for neutral permission and 'could' / 'would you mind' for more formal or polite requests.

grammarNotes.title: Present Perfect for Recent or Ongoing Experience
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Maya dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành để nói về kinh nghiệm của cô: 'I've done a lot of group projects', 'I've been looking at graduate programmes'. Thì này được dùng khi kinh nghiệm đó xảy ra trong quá khứ nhưng vẫn còn liên quan đến hiện tại. So sánh với quá khứ đơn: 'I did a project last year' (đã kết thúc, thời điểm cụ thể) và 'I've done many projects' (kinh nghiệm tổng quát, không cần nêu thời gian).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Maya uses the present perfect to describe her experience: 'I've done a lot of group projects', 'I've been looking at graduate programmes'. This tense is used when past experience is still relevant to the present situation. Compare with the past simple: 'I did a project last year' (specific completed event) vs 'I've done many projects' (general experience, no specific time reference).

translationVi:
Maya: Xin chào. Tôi để ý thấy banner công ty của anh từ bên kia sảnh. Anh có phiền không nếu tôi hỏi một vài câu về các vị trí đang tuyển?
David: Không sao cả — đó chính xác là lý do chúng tôi có mặt ở đây. Chào mừng bạn. Tôi tên David, là trưởng bộ phận tuyển dụng tại Novabridge Solutions. Bạn tên là gì?
Maya: Tôi là Maya. Tôi đang hoàn thành năm cuối ngành quản trị kinh doanh. Tôi đang tìm hiểu các chương trình dành cho sinh viên mới tốt nghiệp, và chương trình của công ty anh đã thu hút sự chú ý của tôi khi xem online.
David: Rất vui được gặp bạn, Maya. Chương trình graduate của chúng tôi là một trong những điều tôi tự hào nhất khi nói đến. Chúng tôi tiếp nhận khoảng hai mươi sinh viên mỗi năm theo ba hướng — vận hành, công nghệ và dịch vụ khách hàng. Bạn có lĩnh vực nào quan tâm không?
Maya: Thực ra tôi quan tâm nhất đến dịch vụ khách hàng. Tôi thích làm việc trực tiếp với con người, và tôi đã làm nhiều dự án nhóm cũng như công việc bán thời gian liên quan đến khách hàng trong thời gian học.
David: Đó là nền tảng tốt cho dịch vụ khách hàng. Tôi có thể hỏi — bạn cảm thấy mình đã học được gì cụ thể từ kinh nghiệm làm việc với khách hàng đó?
Maya: Tôi nghĩ điều lớn nhất là học cách lắng nghe cẩn thận trước khi phản hồi. Nhiều khách hàng không phải lúc nào cũng biết cách mô tả điều họ cần, vì vậy bạn phải đọc hiểu ẩn ý và đặt đúng câu hỏi tiếp theo.
David: Đó là cách nói rất chín chắn. Lắng nghe chủ động là thứ chúng tôi tìm kiếm đặc biệt ở các ứng viên dịch vụ khách hàng. Nhiều ứng viên đánh giá thấp tầm quan trọng của nó so với, chẳng hạn, kiến thức kỹ thuật.
Maya: Nghe điều đó thật nhẹ lòng. Tôi có thể hỏi năm đầu tiên của một sinh viên mới tốt nghiệp trong hướng dịch vụ khách hàng trông như thế nào không?
David: Tất nhiên. Ba tháng đầu là giai đoạn định hướng — bạn theo sát các account manager có kinh nghiệm, tham dự các buổi đào tạo nội bộ, và dần dần tự xử lý các yêu cầu khách hàng nhỏ hơn. Đến tháng thứ sáu, hầu hết các sinh viên mới tốt nghiệp đã quản lý một danh mục tài khoản nhỏ một cách độc lập.
Maya: Nghe như một khởi đầu được tổ chức tốt. Một điều tôi muốn hỏi là về văn hóa công ty. Tôi đã đọc website của anh, nhưng tôi muốn nghe từ người thực sự làm việc ở đó.
David: Thành thật mà nói, tôi sẽ nói văn hóa ở đây mang tính hợp tác nhưng có định hướng. Mọi người ở đây đầy tham vọng, nhưng họ cũng rất sẵn lòng giúp đỡ nhau. Chúng tôi không có văn hóa cạnh tranh nội bộ — mà thiên về việc cả nhóm đạt được mục tiêu chung.
Maya: Đó chính xác là môi trường tôi làm việc tốt nhất. Tôi luôn thấy mình tạo ra kết quả tốt hơn khi có tinh thần tập thể thay vì mọi người cạnh tranh để được công nhận cá nhân.
David: Vậy thì tôi nghĩ bạn sẽ hòa nhập tốt. Một điều cuối — đơn đăng ký của chúng tôi đóng vào ba tuần nữa, vì vậy nếu bạn thực sự muốn ứng tuyển, tôi khuyến khích bạn đừng để quá trễ. Bài kiểm tra viết và phỏng vấn video đòi hỏi phải chuẩn bị.
Maya: Cảm ơn anh rất nhiều, David. Điều này thực sự hữu ích. Tôi chắc chắn sẽ bắt đầu đơn đăng ký trong tuần này.
David: Tuyệt vời. Đây là card của tôi. Hãy thoải mái email cho tôi nếu bạn có câu hỏi khi đã bắt đầu quy trình. Chúc may mắn, Maya.

questions.prompt: Why does Maya approach David's stand at the career fair?
options: She was directed there by a university advisor
She noticed the company's banner and wanted to learn about available roles
She had already applied online and wanted to meet someone in person
She recognised David from a previous networking event
questions.explanation: Maya says: 'I noticed your company's banner from across the hall' and asks to learn about the roles they are hiring for.
questions.hint: Think about what first caught Maya's attention.

questions.prompt: What is Maya's field of study?
questions.explanation: Maya says: 'I'm finishing my final year in business administration.'
questions.hint: She introduces herself and her degree at the beginning of the conversation.

questions.prompt: How many graduates does Novabridge Solutions hire per year?
questions.explanation: David says: 'We take on about twenty graduates each year across three tracks.'
questions.hint: David mentions a specific number when describing the programme.

questions.prompt: Which track is Maya most interested in?
questions.explanation: Maya says: 'I'm most interested in client services, actually.'
questions.hint: She states her preference clearly and explains why.

questions.prompt: According to Maya, what is the biggest skill she gained from customer-facing work?
options: Learning to speak confidently in front of large groups
Understanding how to use business software
Learning to listen carefully and ask the right follow-up questions
Building a professional network of contacts
questions.explanation: Maya says the biggest thing she learned was 'to listen carefully before responding' and 'ask the right follow-up questions.'
questions.hint: She describes what customers often struggle to do, and how she responds.

questions.prompt: What does David say many applicants underestimate?
options: How competitive the application process is
The importance of active listening compared to technical knowledge
How long the induction period lasts
questions.explanation: David says: 'Many applicants underestimate how important it is compared to, say, technical knowledge.' — referring to active listening.
questions.hint: He is responding to what Maya said about listening to customers.

questions.prompt: What happens during the first three months of the graduate programme?
options: Graduates immediately manage their own accounts
Graduates attend university workshops and write reports
Graduates shadow senior staff, attend training, and handle small client queries
Graduates rotate through all three programme tracks
questions.explanation: David explains: 'You shadow experienced account managers, attend internal training sessions, and gradually take on smaller client queries on your own.'
questions.hint: David describes this as the induction period.

questions.prompt: How does David describe the company culture?
options: Relaxed and flexible with few targets
Highly competitive with individual performance bonuses
Collaborative but driven, with shared goals rather than internal competition
Formal and hierarchical with strict reporting structures
questions.explanation: David says the culture is 'collaborative but driven' and 'it's more about the team hitting shared goals.'
questions.hint: He says something specific about internal competition.

questions.prompt: What does Maya say motivates her in a work environment?
options: A sense of collective purpose rather than individual competition
Clear career progression and salary increases
Creative freedom and flexible working hours
questions.explanation: Maya says: 'I produce better results when there's a sense of collective purpose rather than everyone competing for individual recognition.'
questions.hint: She compares two different motivational environments.

options: At the end of the month
questions.explanation: David says: 'Our application closes in three weeks.'
questions.hint: David gives a specific time frame when he urges Maya not to delay.

questions.prompt: What two parts of the application process does David mention require preparation?
options: A group exercise and a presentation
A personality test and a phone interview
A written assessment and a video interview
A CV review and a reference check
questions.explanation: David says: 'The written assessment and video interview take some preparation.'
questions.hint: He warns Maya about these specifically when encouraging her to apply soon.

questions.prompt: What does David offer Maya at the end of the conversation?
options: His business card so she can contact him with questions
A link to an online application form
questions.explanation: David says: 'Here's my card. Feel free to email me if you have any questions once you've started the process.'
questions.hint: He hands her something physical and invites her to be in touch.

cloze.template:
Maya: Hi there. I noticed your company's banner from across the hall. Do you mind if I ask a few questions about the roles you're {{b1}} for?
David: Not at all — that's exactly why we're here. Welcome. My name's David, and I'm a senior recruiter at Novabridge Solutions. What's your name?
Maya: I'm Maya. I'm finishing my final year in business administration. I've been looking at graduate {{b2}}, and yours caught my eye online.
David: Great to meet you, Maya. Our graduate programme is one of the things I'm most proud to talk about. We take on about twenty graduates each year across three tracks — operations, technology, and client services. Do you have a particular area in mind?
Maya: I'm most interested in client services, actually. I enjoy working directly with people, and I've done a lot of group projects and part-time {{b3}} work during my studies.
David: That's a strong background for client services. Can I ask — what do you feel you've gained from that customer-facing experience specifically?
Maya: I think the biggest thing is learning to listen carefully before responding. A lot of customers don't always know how to describe what they need, so you have to read between the lines and ask the right {{b4}} questions.
David: That's a really mature way to put it. Active listening is something we look for specifically in client services candidates. Many applicants {{b5}} how important it is compared to, say, technical knowledge.
Maya: That's reassuring to hear. Can I ask what a typical first year looks like for a graduate in the client services track?
David: Absolutely. The first three months are an {{b6}} period — you shadow experienced account managers, attend internal training sessions, and gradually take on smaller client queries on your own. By month six, most graduates are managing a small portfolio of accounts independently.
Maya: That sounds like a well-structured start. One thing I wanted to ask is about the company culture. I've read your website, but I'd like to hear it from someone who actually works there.
David: Honestly, I'd say the culture is {{b7}} but driven. People here are ambitious, but they're also very willing to help each other out. We don't have a culture of internal competition — it's more about the team hitting shared goals.
Maya: That's the kind of environment I work best in. I've always found that I produce better results when there's a sense of {{b8}} purpose rather than everyone competing for individual recognition.
David: Then I think you'd fit in well. One last thing — our application closes in three weeks, so if you're serious about applying, I'd encourage you not to leave it too late. The written {{b9}} and video interview take some preparation.
Maya: Thank you so much, David. This has been really helpful. I'll definitely start the application this week.
David: Excellent. Here's my card. Feel free to email me if you have any questions once you've started the process. Good luck, Maya.

blanks.explanation: 'Hiring for' is the standard collocation — 'the roles you're hiring for' means the positions the company is currently recruiting to fill.
'Graduate programmes' is the term used in the dialogue — a structured entry-level programme designed for recent university graduates.
'Customer-facing' is the compound adjective used — it describes roles that involve direct interaction with customers.
'Follow-up questions' are questions asked after an initial response to gain further detail or clarification.
'Underestimate' means to value something less than it deserves — David says applicants underestimate the importance of active listening.
'Induction period' is the specific term David uses — it refers to the formal introductory phase for new employees or graduates joining a company.
'Collaborative but driven' is David's exact description of the company culture — 'collaborative' means working together as a team.
'Collective purpose' is the phrase Maya uses — it means a goal or meaning that is held and valued by a group as a whole.
'Written assessment' is the term David uses — it refers to a formal written test given to evaluate a candidate's skills during the application process.

criticalThinkingQuestion: David says that active listening is more important than technical knowledge in client services roles. Do you agree that soft skills like listening can outweigh technical expertise in professional settings? What kinds of jobs demand both equally, and how would you prepare yourself to develop both?
